title: Post-HIIT Full Rest — Beginner
protocol: Your body is showing high stress signals after a HIIT session. NSCA guidelines recommend beginners limit HIIT to 2-3 sessions per week with a maximum 10% weekly progression to avoid overreaching. Your readiness score indicates you have exceeded your current recovery capacity.
actions: Complete rest — no structured exercise today
Sleep 8-9 hours tonight. Research confirms sleep deprivation directly impairs muscle recovery and increases injury risk (Hatia et al., Cureus, 2024)
Drink at least 2.5-3 liters of water throughout the day
Eat a protein-rich meal within 30 minutes — the post-exercise window is peak muscle protein synthesis (ISSN Position Stand, 2017)
Light 10-minute walk only if you feel restless — nothing more
Avoid caffeine after 2 PM to protect sleep quality
return_to_training: Resume light training in 2 days. Do not return to HIIT for at least 3 days per NSCA guidelines.
warning: Beginners who exceed the 10% weekly HIIT progression rule see significantly higher injury rates. Your body adapts during rest, not during training.

title: Post-HIIT Active Recovery — Intermediate
protocol: Your readiness is low after a high-intensity session. Research confirms that active recovery after HIIT helps clear lactate and maintains physiological adaptation without adding training stress (Menz et al., Frontiers in Physiology, 2018). Avoid all high-intensity work today.
actions: Active recovery only — 20-minute easy walk or gentle cycling below 60% max HR
Foam roll major muscle groups for 10-15 minutes to promote blood flow
Stretch hip flexors, hamstrings, and shoulders — hold each 30-60 seconds
Hydrate with 2.5-3 liters of water minimum
Prioritize 7-8 hours of sleep — systematic reviews confirm sleep is the single most powerful recovery intervention for athletes (Sports Medicine Open, 2023)
Consider a cold shower if muscles feel acutely sore
return_to_training: Light training tomorrow. Return to HIIT intensity in 2 days.
warning: Consecutive HIIT sessions without recovery leads to non-functional overreaching, which can require weeks to reverse (Meeusen et al., European Journal of Sport Science).
sources: Menz et al., Frontiers in Physiology 2018

title: Post-HIIT Recovery — Advanced Athlete
protocol: Even advanced athletes need recovery. Research on overtraining syndrome distinguishes functional overreaching (recovers in under 2 weeks) from non-functional overreaching (up to 4 weeks) — acting on low readiness now prevents the latter (Carrard et al., Sports Health, 2022).
actions: Active recovery session only — 30-minute easy swim or cycling at 50% effort
Mobility work: 20 minutes focusing on problem areas
Nutrition: 1.6-2g protein per kg of body weight supports muscle protein synthesis (ISSN Position Stand)
Sleep 7-9 hours — growth hormone peaks during deep sleep stages
Review your weekly volume — a deload (50% volume reduction) may be due
Monitor resting HR tomorrow — if still elevated 5+ BPM above baseline, extend recovery
return_to_training: Moderate intensity tomorrow. Full HIIT intensity in 2 days only if readiness improves.
warning: Advanced athletes who chronically ignore low readiness signals are the primary population diagnosed with overtraining syndrome (OTS), which can sideline performance for months.

title: Post-Strength Full Rest — Beginner
protocol: Muscle protein synthesis after resistance training requires 48-72 hours in the same muscle group for beginners. Your low readiness confirms full rest is needed today.
actions: Complete rest from all resistance training
Light walking is acceptable — keep under 20 minutes
Protein: 0.8-1.2g per kg of body weight supports muscle repair
Hydrate: 2-2.5 liters of water minimum
Sleep 8-9 hours — sleep deprivation reduces muscle recovery significantly (Frontiers in Physiology, 2025)
Gentle stretching only — no loading
return_to_training: Do not train the same muscle groups for at least 48 hours.
warning: Beginners have lower neuromuscular adaptation — their muscles need more recovery time than intermediate or advanced athletes for the same session.
sources: Frontiers in Physiology 2025 — Sleep Deprivation and Sports Performance

title: Post-Strength Active Recovery — Intermediate
protocol: Readiness is low after strength work. Central nervous system fatigue from heavy lifting accumulates beyond what is visible as muscle soreness. Switch muscle groups or do active recovery today.
actions: Switch to opposing muscle groups — use upper/lower or push/pull split
Or: 20-minute light cardio at conversational pace (below 65% max HR)
Foam roll trained muscle groups for 10 minutes
Protein intake: 1.4-1.7g per kg body weight today
Hydrate well: 2.5-3 liters
7-8 hours sleep minimum
return_to_training: Same muscle group in 48 hours.
warning: Heavy compound movements create significant central nervous system fatigue beyond what is felt in the muscles — low readiness after these sessions is a serious signal.

title: Post-Strength Deload — Advanced
protocol: Low readiness in an advanced strength athlete typically signals a deload is needed. Deload weeks (40-50% volume reduction) are evidence-based periodization tools that preserve strength while allowing full systemic recovery.
actions: Reduce volume by 40-50% if training today
Keep intensity moderate — no PR attempts
Prioritize mobility and activation work
Prioritize carbohydrates for glycogen replenishment alongside protein
Sleep 7-9 hours — non-negotiable
Consider formal deload week every 4-6 training weeks
return_to_training: Reduced intensity for 2-3 days before resuming full load.
warning: Advanced athletes accumulate fatigue across training blocks invisibly — performance plateaus are often the first sign that recovery has been insufficient for weeks.

title: Post-Cardio Rest — Beginner
protocol: For beginners, even moderate cardio creates significant aerobic adaptation demand. Rest today allows physiological changes from your last session to consolidate.
actions: Rest completely from structured exercise
Light walking only if needed — under 15 minutes
Hydrate well: 2.5-3 liters of water
Replenish carbohydrates — glycogen stores are depleted after cardio sessions
Sleep 8-9 hours
Elevate legs for 10-15 minutes to reduce lower body fatigue
return_to_training: Light cardio in 2 days. Keep intensity below 60% max HR.
warning: Beginners who overtrain cardio experience burnout and motivation loss — rest is when aerobic fitness actually improves through physiological adaptation.
sources: ACSM Guidelines for Exercise Testing and Prescription

title: Post-Cardio Active Recovery — Intermediate
protocol: Consecutive moderate-high cardio sessions elevate cortisol and impair fat metabolism when insufficient recovery occurs. Your low readiness confirms you need easy movement only today.
actions: Easy 20-minute walk or gentle yoga only
Focus on slow controlled breathing — activates parasympathetic recovery
Carbohydrate-rich meal to restore glycogen: rice, fruit, bread
Hydrate: 2.5-3 liters minimum with electrolytes if sweating was heavy
Compression garments can help reduce lower limb soreness
Sleep 7-8 hours
return_to_training: Easy cardio (Zone 1-2) tomorrow. Moderate intensity in 2 days.
warning: Inadequate recovery between cardio sessions elevates cortisol and impairs the aerobic adaptations you are training for.
sources: Sports Medicine Post-Exercise Recovery Narrative Review 2025

title: Post-Cardio Recovery — Advanced Endurance Athlete
protocol: Research highlights that endurance athletes often feel subjectively recovered before physiological recovery is complete (Mann et al., European Journal of Sport Science, 2024). Your readiness score gives you the objective signal beyond subjective perception.
actions: Easy Zone 1-2 movement only — 20-30 minutes maximum
Keep heart rate below 65% of max
Prioritize carbohydrate and electrolyte replenishment
Sleep is your highest-priority recovery tool today
Consider massage or foam rolling for lower body
Review your weekly mileage — acute volume spikes are a primary overuse injury predictor
return_to_training: Easy training tomorrow. Hard efforts in 2-3 days.
warning: The disconnect between perceived recovery and physiological reality is well-documented in endurance athletes — trust your readiness score over subjective feel (Mann et al., 2024).
sources: Mann et al., European Journal of Sport Science 2024
Walsh et al., British Journal of Sports Medicine 2021

title: Post-Yoga Recovery
protocol: Even yoga creates physiological stress — eccentric holds and deep stretching create real muscular demand. Low readiness after yoga typically reflects accumulated systemic fatigue combined with the session.
actions: Rest from structured exercise today
Gentle walking only
Focus on hydration: 2-2.5 liters
Light protein meal to support muscle repair
Sleep 8 hours minimum
Practice breathing or meditation — promotes parasympathetic nervous system recovery
return_to_training: Gentle yoga or light movement tomorrow.
warning: Low readiness after yoga often reflects systemic fatigue rather than yoga-specific load — look at your overall week, not just today.

title: Moderate Readiness Post-HIIT — Beginner
protocol: You have moderate readiness. You can train today but intensity must be controlled. NSCA guidelines recommend beginners never perform back-to-back HIIT sessions.
actions: Light cardio only — 20-30 minutes at 60-65% max HR
Or: bodyweight mobility circuit at low effort
Avoid all high-intensity intervals today
Hydrate: 2-2.5 liters
Balanced meals with carbs and protein
Sleep 7-8 hours tonight to consolidate readiness for tomorrow
return_to_training: HIIT intensity tomorrow if readiness improves.
warning: Moderate readiness means partial recovery — treating it as full readiness is the most common pathway to gradual overreaching.

title: Moderate Readiness Post-HIIT — Intermediate
protocol: Moderate readiness allows structured training at reduced intensity. Use this session for aerobic base building rather than peak performance.
actions: Moderate intensity session — 70% of normal effort
Reduce interval count — remove one round from your normal program
Or: strength training at moderate weight (70-75% 1RM)
Hydrate: 2.5 liters
Post-workout protein within 30 minutes — peak muscle protein synthesis window (ISSN 2017)
7-8 hours sleep
return_to_training: Full intensity in 1-2 days depending on tomorrow's readiness.
warning: Use moderate readiness days for aerobic base at 70% effort — this produces adaptation without adding recovery debt.

title: Moderate Readiness Strength — Intermediate
protocol: You can train strength today at moderate volume and intensity. Attempting maximal efforts when readiness is moderate significantly increases acute injury risk.
actions: Train at 70-80% of your normal working weight
Reduce sets by 1-2 from your normal program
Focus on form and mind-muscle connection
Warm up thoroughly: 10-15 minutes
Post-workout: carbohydrate + protein combination enhances glycogen recovery (Sports Medicine, 2025)
7-8 hours sleep tonight
return_to_training: Full intensity training in 1-2 days.
warning: Attempting personal records on moderate readiness days is a documented cause of acute muscle strains — save max efforts for high readiness days.

title: Moderate Readiness Strength — Advanced
protocol: Advanced athletes can use moderate readiness days for hypertrophy work, technique refinement, or accessory movements. This is not a day for heavy compound lifts.
actions: Hypertrophy focus: higher reps, moderate weight (65-75% 1RM)
Prioritize accessory and weak-point training
Avoid near-maximal singles or heavy compound work
Thorough warm-up including activation drills
Protein: 1.6-2g per kg body weight — maximizes muscle protein synthesis
Sleep 7-9 hours
return_to_training: Full strength intensity in 1-2 days.
warning: Advanced athletes who consistently push through moderate readiness accumulate fatigue across training blocks — this is the pathway to overtraining syndrome.

title: Moderate Readiness Cardio
protocol: Moderate readiness is appropriate for Zone 2 cardio (60-70% max HR) — this is the aerobic base-building zone that produces adaptation without adding recovery stress.
actions: Easy to moderate cardio — 25-35 minutes in Zone 2
Keep HR between 60-70% of max throughout
Use the talk test: you should be able to hold a conversation
Hydrate before, during, and after: 500ml per 30 minutes of exercise
Carbohydrates 1-2 hours before if training feels necessary
Sleep 7-8 hours tonight
return_to_training: Moderate-high intensity in 1-2 days depending on readiness.
warning: Zone 2 cardio on moderate readiness days is productive — pushing into Zone 4-5 creates accumulated fatigue without proportional adaptation.

title: High Readiness HIIT — Beginner
protocol: Your body is well recovered and ready for training. NSCA recommends maintaining 10% weekly volume increases maximum to prevent overreaching, even when feeling great.
actions: HIIT session at your planned intensity — 20-30 minutes
Warm up 10 minutes before starting intervals
Keep intervals to your program — do not add extra sets
Cool down 10 minutes after session
Post-workout nutrition within 30 minutes: protein + carbohydrates
Sleep 8 hours to convert today's training stimulus into adaptation
return_to_training: Rest or active recovery tomorrow — no consecutive HIIT sessions.
warning: High readiness is not an invitation to overtrain. NSCA guidelines: beginners need 48 hours between HIIT sessions regardless of perceived readiness.

title: High Readiness HIIT — Intermediate
protocol: Excellent readiness. HIIT at 85-95% max HR on high readiness days produces the largest aerobic gains. This is your day to push intensity with a clear physiological rationale.
actions: Full HIIT session at planned intensity — 85-95% max HR during intervals
Can add one extra interval if feeling strong — no more
Thorough warm-up: 10-12 minutes
Track performance metrics — this is a benchmark session
Post-workout: protein + fast carbohydrates within 30 minutes
Sleep 7-8 hours to convert stimulus into adaptation
return_to_training: Active recovery or rest tomorrow.
warning: High readiness days are for quality training — execute your plan precisely, not junk volume.

title: High Readiness Strength — Intermediate
protocol: Your readiness is high. Progressive overload applied to well-recovered muscles is the primary driver of strength gains — this is your day to apply it.
actions: Full strength session at planned weights
Progressive overload: add 2.5-5kg if your program allows
Warm up with activation sets: 40%, 60%, 80% of working weight
Focus on compound movements: squat, hinge, press, pull
Post-workout: 20-40g protein within 30-45 minutes (ISSN evidence-based range)
Sleep 7-8 hours for maximum overnight muscle protein synthesis
return_to_training: Allow 48 hours before training the same muscle group.
warning: Even on high readiness days, form breakdown under fatigue causes injury — prioritize technical execution over weight on the bar.

title: High Readiness Strength — Advanced
protocol: Peak readiness for an advanced athlete. Advanced periodization models designate high-readiness days for maximum effort, volume peaks, and PR attempts — this is that day.
actions: Train at full intensity — this is your performance day
PR attempts are appropriate if properly warmed up and programmed
Full planned volume — no reductions
Pre-workout: carbohydrates 2-3 hours before for glycogen availability
Intra-workout hydration and electrolytes for sessions over 60 minutes
Post-workout: immediate protein + carbs co-ingestion maximizes glycogen resynthesis (Sports Medicine, 2025)
Sleep 8-9 hours — advanced athletes need more recovery after maximum effort
return_to_training: Deload or reduced intensity for 1-2 days after maximum effort.
warning: Save maximum efforts for high readiness days — this is the core principle of optimal periodization and the key differentiator of advanced athletes.
sources: NSCA Advanced Periodization and Peaking Guidelines

title: High Readiness Cardio — Intermediate
protocol: High readiness for cardio training. Your aerobic system is recovered and primed for quality work. Use this session for threshold or tempo efforts that drive the largest aerobic fitness improvements.
actions: Moderate to high intensity cardio — 35-50 minutes
Include tempo efforts: 20-30 minutes at 75-85% max HR
Warm up 10 minutes before intensity work
Cool down 10 minutes after
Hydrate throughout: 500ml per 30 minutes of exercise
Carbohydrate-rich meal 2 hours before for glycogen availability
return_to_training: Easy cardio (Zone 1-2) or rest tomorrow.
warning: High readiness cardio days are for quality efforts — accumulating extra easy mileage adds recovery debt without aerobic benefit.

title: Sleep and Athletic Recovery — Evidence-Based Protocol
protocol: Sleep is the single most powerful recovery intervention for athletes. A 2024 narrative review (Hatia et al., Cureus) confirms that inadequate sleep negatively affects physical strength, endurance, cognitive function, and injury risk.
actions: 7-9 hours of sleep is the evidence-based target for athletes (Walsh et al., British Journal of Sports Medicine, 2021)
Consistent sleep and wake times improve sleep quality — irregular schedules disrupt circadian rhythm
Avoid screens 30-60 minutes before sleep — blue light delays melatonin release
Keep bedroom cool (18-20 degrees Celsius) — optimal for deep sleep onset
Avoid caffeine after 2 PM — caffeine has a 5-6 hour half-life
Adenosine accumulation from extended wakefulness directly heightens perceived exertion the following day (Frontiers in Physiology, 2025)
return_to_training: N/A — universal guidance.
warning: Chronic sleep deprivation below 6 hours reduces testosterone, elevates cortisol, and impairs muscle recovery and cognitive function (Frontiers in Physiology, 2025).
sources: Walsh et al., British Journal of Sports Medicine 2021

title: Hydration Protocol — Evidence-Based
protocol: Even mild dehydration of 2% body weight impairs physical and cognitive performance. Proper hydration is a primary driver of recovery speed and next-day readiness.
actions: Baseline: 35ml per kg of body weight per day
Add 500-750ml per hour of moderate exercise
Add 750ml-1L per hour of intense exercise
Monitor urine color — pale yellow indicates adequate hydration
Electrolytes are essential for sessions over 60 minutes
Hydrate before you feel thirsty — thirst signals early dehydration has already begun
return_to_training: N/A — universal guidance.
warning: Athletes training in high heat and humidity face significantly elevated fluid loss — this directly impacts recovery speed and next-day readiness scores.

title: Overtraining Syndrome — Warning Signs and Protocol
protocol: Overtraining syndrome (OTS) develops when training stress consistently exceeds recovery capacity. Research distinguishes functional overreaching (under 2 weeks recovery), non-functional overreaching (up to 4 weeks), and full OTS (months). Acting on persistent low readiness prevents progression to OTS (Carrard et al., Sports Health, 2022).
actions: Warning: persistent low readiness for 5 or more consecutive days
Warning: resting HR elevated 5+ BPM above personal baseline — confirmed OTS predictor
Warning: declining performance despite consistent training
Warning: persistent muscle soreness that does not resolve within 72 hours
Warning: mood changes, irritability, disrupted sleep
If 3 or more warning signs present — take a full deload week: 40-50% volume reduction
return_to_training: Full deload week before resuming normal training load.
warning: Overtraining syndrome can require months to reverse. IOC 2023 consensus identifies OTS as a serious condition requiring professional evaluation if symptoms persist after a deload week.
sources: Meeusen et al. OTS European Consensus

title: Elevated Resting Heart Rate — Recovery Protocol
protocol: An elevated resting heart rate above personal baseline is one of the most scientifically validated OTS indicators. Research confirms a 5+ BPM elevation is a strong overtraining predictor, with overtrained runners averaging a 10 BPM resting HR increase.
actions: Resting HR 5-7 BPM above baseline: reduce training intensity by 30%
Resting HR 8-10 BPM above baseline: active recovery only
Resting HR 10+ BPM above baseline: complete rest day required
Focus on parasympathetic recovery: slow diaphragmatic breathing, gentle walking
Eliminate stimulants: caffeine and pre-workout supplements
Prioritize sleep and hydration above all other interventions
return_to_training: Return to training when resting HR is within 3 BPM of personal baseline.
warning: Persistently elevated resting HR across multiple days is a reliable early warning of overtraining syndrome — do not override this signal.
sources: Wu Tsai Human Performance Alliance — HRV and Overtraining Review 2024
Sports medicine literature citing 1985 and 1992 HR overtraining studies

title: Post-Workout Nutrition — Evidence-Based Protocol
protocol: The 30-minute post-exercise window is the established golden window of recovery where muscle protein synthesis is at peak and carbohydrate + protein intake most effectively accelerates recovery (Frontiers in Nutrition, 2025; ISSN Position Stand, 2017).
actions: 20-40g protein within 30-45 minutes post-training — ISSN evidence-based range
Include fast-digesting carbohydrates: fruit, white rice, bread to restore glycogen
Endurance athletes: 3:1 carbohydrate to protein ratio for glycogen recovery
Strength athletes: 2:1 carbohydrate to protein ratio supports glycogen and muscle repair
Rehydrate: 500ml-1L of water post-workout minimum
Avoid high-fat meals immediately post-workout — slows nutrient absorption
return_to_training: N/A — nutrition guidance.
warning: Skipping post-workout nutrition significantly impairs next-day readiness. ISSN confirms not eating after training provides no recovery benefit compared to timely nutritional intake.
